perturbation.original_text: Unless a shorter period is stated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not assert any claims against the receiving party for disclosures of Confidential Information made more than five (5) years from the date of the CITR.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a shorter period is stated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not assert any claims against the receiving party for disclosures of Confidential Information made more than one (1) year from the date of the CITR.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period for asserting claims regarding confidential information disclosures to one year may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable in California, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 339 dictates a two-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract, obligation or liability not founded upon an instrument of writing; it also dictates a three-year statute of limitations for actions based on fraud or mistake. Setting the time to assert a claim to 1 year is a violation of the statute of limitations.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 339. Within two years: 1. An action upon a contract, obligation or liability not founded upon an instrument of writing, except as provided in Section 2725 of the Commercial Code or subdivision 2 of Section 337 of this code; or an action founded upon a contract, obligation or liability, evidenced by a certificate, or abstract or guaranty of title of real property, or by a policy of title insurance; provided, that the cause of action upon a contract, obligation or liability evidenced by a certificate, or abstract or guaranty of title of real property or policy of title insurance shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery of the loss or damage suffered by the aggrieved party thereunder. 2. An action against a sheriff or coroner upon a liability incurred by the doing of an act in an official capacity and in virtue of office, or by the omission of an official duty including the nonpayment of money collected in the enforcement of a judgment. 3. An action based up
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the claim assertion period for confidential information disclosures from five years to one year. The Law Explanation states that CCP § 339 dictates a two-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract, obligation or liability not founded upon an instrument of writing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms this, stating that CCP 339 provides a two-year limit for "[a]n action upon a contract, obligation or liability not founded upon an instrument of writing". Therefore, the changed text creates a legal contradiction because it sets a one-year limit, violating the legally mandated two-year statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall promptly return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the disclosing party along with all copies which it made.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event this Agreement is terminated, and the disclosing party so requests, the receiving party shall, at its convenience, return or destroy (and certify destruction of) all Confidential Information which it received from the disclosing party along with all copies which it made.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'at its convenience' for returning or destroying confidential information after termination introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with California's trade secret laws, which require reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. Changing 'promptly' to 'at its convenience' weakens the protection of trade secrets, making compliance with the law regarding trade secret misappropriation less certain, since promptness is consistent with the 'reasonable efforts' standard under trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly' to 'at its convenience' introduces an inconsistency regarding the time frame for returning or destroying confidential information, potentially weakening trade secret protection. The Law Explanation suggests this shift undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard typically associated with trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, defines misappropriation and mentions the duty to maintain secrecy. While the snippet does not explicitly define 'promptly,' the modification introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the expected standard of care for protecting trade secrets, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written approval of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party, except as required by subpoena, in which case only notification is required.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows disclosure of confidential information based solely on a subpoena, potentially violating California's requirements for protecting trade secrets and confidential information even when faced with legal process.
perturbation.law_explanation: While a subpoena requires a response, California law doesn't automatically allow the disclosure of confidential information solely based on it. CCP § 2017.310 covers the manner of service of a subpoena, but it doesn't address confidentiality obligations when responding to it. The law requires a reasonable effort to maintain confidentiality and seek a protective order.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 2. Scope of Discovery [2017.010 - 2017.320] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) ARTICLE 3. Violation of the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act [2017.310 - 2017.320] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2017.310. (a) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, it is the policy of the State of California that confidential settlement agreements are disfavored in any civil action the factual foundation for which establishes a cause of action for a violation of the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act (Chapter 11(commencing with Section 15600) of Part 3 of Division 9 of the Welfare and Institutions Code). (b) Provisions of a confidential settlement agreement described in subdivision (a) may not be recognized or enforced by the court absent a showing of any of the following: (1) The information is privileged under existing law. (2) The information is not evidence of abuse of an elder or dependent adult, as described in Sections 15610.30, 15610.57, and 15610.63 of the Welfare an
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an exception to the confidentiality obligation based solely on a subpoena. The Law Explanation states that California law doesn't automatically allow disclosure of confidential information solely based on a subpoena and requires reasonable effort to maintain confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 2017.310, primarily addresses settlement agreements related to elder abuse and does not directly contradict the change, but it shows the level of specificity the law requires, and therefore the modified clause potentially weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Delaware could be problematic if the parties are primarily located and operating in California, potentially conflicting with California's strong public policy interests in protecting its businesses and employees, particularly concerning trade secrets and non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements in California. Delaware law may be more permissive regarding non-compete clauses, so changing the governing law to Delaware could allow for the enforcement of a non-compete clause that would be invalid in California, thus conflicting with California's public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law may be more permissive regarding non-compete clauses, whereas California law generally prohibits them, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which quotes Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600, stating that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The change in governing law, therefore, directly contradicts California's established public policy against non-compete agreements, potentially allowing for the enforcement of a clause that would be invalid in California.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with at least the same degree of care that the receiving party uses to protect its own similar categories of confidential and proprietary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall maintain the Confidential Information with the degree of care it deems appropriate, considering its existing resources and business needs.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, potentially violating California's requirements for protecting trade secrets by failing to implement adequate security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1(a) allows for injunctive relief against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing the receiving party to determine the level of care based on its own resources and needs, the agreement weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, and may not meet a reasonable standard of care necessary to prevent misappropriation, potentially leading to legal action and conflicting with the intent of trade secret protection laws.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 3426.1(a) concerning the misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' for protecting confidential information, aligning with the legal requirement to prevent misappropriation. The changed text allows the receiving party to determine the level of care based on its own resources and needs, potentially falling below a reasonable standard. Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy which the changed text weakens. Therefore, the modification weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets and may not meet a reasonable standard of care, creating a contradiction.
